**Josh Koscheck** (born November 30, 1977) is an American retired mixed martial artist who fought in the [Welterweight division](/wiki/Welterweight_(MMA) "Welterweight (MMA)") of the [Ultimate Fighting Championship](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship") (UFC) and [Bellator MMA](/wiki/Bellator_MMA "Bellator MMA"). Koscheck made his debut with the UFC on the [first season](/wiki/The_Ultimate_Fighter_1 "The Ultimate Fighter 1") of the [reality television](/wiki/Reality_television "Reality television") show *[The Ultimate Fighter](/wiki/The_Ultimate_Fighter "The Ultimate Fighter")* as a [middleweight](/wiki/Middleweight_(MMA) "Middleweight (MMA)"), and had since fought for the company 25 times from 2005–2015, compiling a UFC record of 15–10 and having contended for the [UFC Welterweight Championship](/wiki/UFC_Welterweight_Championship "UFC Welterweight Championship") once.

## Mixed martial arts career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Josh_Koscheck&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Mixed martial arts career")]

### Ultimate Fighting Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Josh_Koscheck&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ultimate Fighting Championship")]

Prior to his [MMA](/wiki/Mixed_martial_arts "Mixed martial arts") career, Koscheck was an accomplished [wrestler](/wiki/Scholastic_wrestling "Scholastic wrestling") at [Waynesburg Central High School](/wiki/Waynesburg_Central_High_School "Waynesburg Central High School"), graduating in 1997. He attended [Edinboro University](/wiki/Edinboro_University "Edinboro University"), where he won the 174-pound [national championship](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Wrestling_Championships "NCAA Division I Wrestling Championships") in 2001 as a [senior](/wiki/Senior_(education) "Senior (education)"), and was a four-time [NCAA](/wiki/NCAA "NCAA") [Division 1](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") [All-American](/wiki/All-America "All-America") [collegiate wrestler](/wiki/Collegiate_wrestling "Collegiate wrestling").

Koscheck appeared on the [first season](/wiki/The_Ultimate_Fighter_1 "The Ultimate Fighter 1") of [The Ultimate Fighter](/wiki/The_Ultimate_Fighter "The Ultimate Fighter") as a [middleweight](/wiki/Middleweight_(MMA) "Middleweight (MMA)") fighter. He defeated [Chris Leben](/wiki/Chris_Leben "Chris Leben") via unanimous decision to advance to the semifinals, where he was defeated by [Diego Sanchez](/wiki/Diego_Sanchez "Diego Sanchez") via split decision. Koscheck had his first official UFC fight on April 9, 2005, at [The Ultimate Finale](/wiki/The_Ultimate_Fighter_1_Finale "The Ultimate Fighter 1 Finale"), where he defeated [Chris Sanford](/wiki/Chris_Sanford "Chris Sanford") with a first-round KO. Koscheck followed this victory with another win at [UFC Ultimate Fight Night](/wiki/2005_in_UFC#UFC_Ultimate_Fight_Night "2005 in UFC") over [Pete Spratt](/wiki/Pete_Spratt "Pete Spratt") in Koscheck's debut at welterweight. Since then, Koscheck has amassed an additional three victories after experiencing his first career loss to [Drew Fickett](/wiki/Drew_Fickett "Drew Fickett") due to a rear naked choke. Koscheck then defeated [Jonathan Goulet](/wiki/Jonathan_Goulet "Jonathan Goulet") at *[UFC Fight Night 6](/wiki/UFC_Fight_Night_6 "UFC Fight Night 6")* by submission due to strikes.

In 2006, Koscheck began training at the [American Kickboxing Academy](/wiki/American_Kickboxing_Academy "American Kickboxing Academy") (AKA) in [San Jose, California](/wiki/San_Jose,_California "San Jose, California"), to which he accredits his vastly improved kickboxing technique.

Koscheck's fight on December 13, 2006 was against [Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_Jiu-Jitsu "Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu") black belt Jeff Joslin at *[UFC Fight Night 7](/wiki/UFC_Fight_Night_7 "UFC Fight Night 7")* where he won by unanimous decision.

Koscheck was then scheduled to fight [Diego Sanchez](/wiki/Diego_Sanchez "Diego Sanchez") on April 7, 2007 at *[UFC 69](/wiki/UFC_69 "UFC 69")*. This was the second encounter between the two Ultimate Fighter 1 alumni, now fighting as welterweights. The first fight – which did not count towards Koscheck's official record due to the bout being considered an exhibition match for *The Ultimate Fighter* television taping – resulted in a split decision win for Sanchez. At the weigh-ins for the event, Sanchez shoved Koscheck during the traditional post-weigh-in staredown. Although Sanchez continued to shout at Koscheck, no conflict ensued. In their fight, Koscheck defeated Sanchez by unanimous decision (30-27, 30-27, 30-27). Koscheck outstruck Sanchez throughout all three rounds and only shot in for a takedown once. During his post-fight interview, Koscheck repeatedly exclaimed, "Nineteen-and-one," in reference to Sanchez' first mixed martial arts defeat (including the two fights from *The Ultimate Fighter* show). UFC President [Dana White](/wiki/Dana_White "Dana White") announced a few weeks after the fight that Diego Sanchez had an undiagnosed illness before and during the fight which turned out to be a serious staph infection.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

On August 25, 2007, Koscheck was defeated by [Georges St-Pierre](/wiki/Georges_St-Pierre "Georges St-Pierre") at *[UFC 74](/wiki/UFC_74 "UFC 74")* via unanimous decision (30–27, 29–28, 29–28). St-Pierre's wrestling skills were displayed during the match by outwrestling Koscheck, scoring takedowns, stuffing Koscheck's takedown attempts and maintaining top position throughout most of the fight. There was speculation before the fight that Koscheck would outmatch St-Pierre on the ground due to his credentials, but St-Pierre was confident that he was a better wrestler and striker and was more well-versed in submissions than Koscheck.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Koscheck would rebound with a second round TKO victory over [Dustin Hazelett](/wiki/Dustin_Hazelett "Dustin Hazelett") at [UFC 82](/wiki/UFC_82 "UFC 82").

Koscheck then faced [Chris Lytle](/wiki/Chris_Lytle "Chris Lytle") at [UFC 86](/wiki/UFC_86 "UFC 86"), winning by unanimous decision after repeatedly taking Lytle down and cutting him badly with "ground and pound" elbows.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Koscheck then agreed to step in and fight [Thiago Alves](/wiki/Thiago_Alves_(fighter) "Thiago Alves (fighter)") at [UFC 90](/wiki/UFC_90 "UFC 90") on just 2 weeks notice due to an injury suffered by [Diego Sanchez](/wiki/Diego_Sanchez "Diego Sanchez"). Koscheck lost the fight via unanimous decision. Alves landed powerful leg kicks and managed to drop Koscheck more than once during the fight.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

On December 10, 2008, Koscheck fought [Yoshiyuki Yoshida](/wiki/Yoshiyuki_Yoshida "Yoshiyuki Yoshida") in the main event at [UFC: Fight for the Troops](/wiki/UFC:_Fight_for_the_Troops "UFC: Fight for the Troops") and won by KO in the first round by landing two devastating straight right punches to Yoshida's head. The Japanese fighter remained on the canvas, while being tended to by commission doctors and was removed from the Octagon on a stretcher as a precautionary measure. He was later treated and released from a local hospital for a concussion. Koscheck received $30,000 bonus from the [UFC](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship") for *Knockout of the Night*.[[7]](#cite_note-UFCTroops-7)

Koscheck fought promotional newcomer [Paulo Thiago](/wiki/Paulo_Thiago "Paulo Thiago") at [UFC 95](/wiki/UFC_95 "UFC 95").[[8]](#cite_note-8) Koscheck was defeated by the debuting Brazilian via first-round KO. Thiago hit Koscheck squarely with a right uppercut and landed a left hook as Koscheck fell to the canvas in a stupor. After the bout was halted, Koscheck got up and argued that he was still alert and able to defend himself. Shortly after the stoppage, Koscheck was seen calmly offering his opinion of the stoppage to the referee: "Too early - just a little bit too early."

Koscheck was to fight [Chris Wilson](/wiki/Chris_Wilson_(fighter) "Chris Wilson (fighter)") at [UFC 98](/wiki/UFC_98 "UFC 98") but was forced to withdraw after suffering a foot injury in training. He returned at [UFC 103](/wiki/UFC_103 "UFC 103") and defeated returning veteran [Frank Trigg](/wiki/Frank_Trigg "Frank Trigg") via TKO (punches) in the 1st round.

Koscheck was expected to face [Mike Pierce](/wiki/Mike_Pierce "Mike Pierce") on January 11, 2010, at [UFC Fight Night 20](/wiki/UFC_Fight_Night_20 "UFC Fight Night 20"),[[9]](#cite_note-9) but instead went on to face [Anthony Johnson](/wiki/Anthony_Johnson_(fighter) "Anthony Johnson (fighter)") on November 21, 2009, at [UFC 106](/wiki/UFC_106 "UFC 106").

On November 21, 2009 at [UFC 106](/wiki/UFC_106 "UFC 106"), Koscheck defeated [Anthony Johnson](/wiki/Anthony_Johnson_(fighter) "Anthony Johnson (fighter)") by rear naked choke at 4:47 in the 2nd round. In the first round, Koscheck blocked a knee aimed at his head while he had two knees on the ground. Koscheck went to the ground holding his face, prompting the referee to step in and deduct a point from Johnson. Commentators accused Koscheck of faking the impact of the knee, which seemed to miss Koscheck's face on the replays. Koscheck has said in multiple interviews that he was reacting to an eye poke, not the knee.[[10]](#cite_note-10) In round two, Koscheck poked Johnson in the eye twice leading to Johnson being given time to recover. Because of this, many fans think due to the controversy in both rounds between the fighters, it should've been declared a No Contest. In his post fight interview, he called out [Dan Hardy](/wiki/Dan_Hardy "Dan Hardy"), the #1 contender in the Welterweight Division. This fight earned him two $75,000 awards for *[Fight of the Night](/wiki/List_of_UFC_bonus_award_recipients "List of UFC bonus award recipients")* and *[Submission of the Night](/wiki/List_of_UFC_bonus_award_recipients "List of UFC bonus award recipients")* totaling $140,000.[[11]](#cite_note-UFC106-11)

Koscheck was scheduled to rematch [Paulo Thiago](/wiki/Paulo_Thiago "Paulo Thiago") at [UFC 109](/wiki/UFC_109 "UFC 109"), but was forced off the card with an injury and was replaced by [AKA](/wiki/American_Kickboxing_Academy "American Kickboxing Academy") teammate [Mike Swick](/wiki/Mike_Swick "Mike Swick").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

#### Welterweight contendership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Josh_Koscheck&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Welterweight contendership")]

Koscheck was signed to fight [Paul Daley](/wiki/Paul_Daley "Paul Daley") at [UFC 113](/wiki/UFC_113 "UFC 113"), which he won by unanimous decision. The fight however, was not without controversy as similar to his fight with Anthony Johnson, Koscheck faked being hit with an illegal knee, resulting in a point deduction for Daley. After the final bell, Daley walked up to Koscheck and delivered a sucker punch to Koscheck, resulting in him being restrained by the referee. In an interview at the conclusion of the event, Dana White announced that Daley would never fight in the UFC again. White was quoted as saying, "He will never come back, I do not care if he's the best 170-pounder in the world. He's never coming back here. There's no excuse for that. You never hit a guy like that after the bell." Via [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter"), Koscheck expressed a desire to face [Tito Ortiz](/wiki/Tito_Ortiz "Tito Ortiz") at a catchweight after his fight with Daley, in large part because Ortiz poked fun at [Mark Coleman](/wiki/Mark_Coleman "Mark Coleman") after he was defeated at [UFC 109](/wiki/UFC_109 "UFC 109").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In 2010, Koscheck was a coach on [The Ultimate Fighter](/wiki/The_Ultimate_Fighter "The Ultimate Fighter") season 12. The season 12 finale aired on December 4, 2010 with the main event consisting of two fighters ([Michael Johnson](/wiki/Michael_Johnson_(fighter) "Michael Johnson (fighter)") and [Jonathan Brookins](/wiki/Jonathan_Brookins "Jonathan Brookins")) from team [Georges St-Pierre](/wiki/Georges_St-Pierre "Georges St-Pierre").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Koscheck faced [Georges St-Pierre](/wiki/Georges_St-Pierre "Georges St-Pierre") in a rematch at [UFC 124](/wiki/UFC_124 "UFC 124") for the [UFC Welterweight Championship](/wiki/UFC_Welterweight_Championship "UFC Welterweight Championship"). UFC 124 was heavily hyped due to Josh's well known animosity towards St-Pierre, stemming from his loss by decision in their fight at [UFC 74](/wiki/UFC_74 "UFC 74"). St Pierre utilized his left jab effectively in the bout, winning all rounds to defeat Koscheck 50-45 by unanimous decision.[[15]](#cite_note-15) This fight earned him a $100,000 *[Fight of the Night](/wiki/List_of_UFC_bonus_award_recipients "List of UFC bonus award recipients")* award.[[16]](#cite_note-UFC124-16)

Koscheck suffered a broken [orbital bone](/wiki/Orbital_bone "Orbital bone") on the right side of his face during the fight with St-Pierre. Since he was unable to fly due to air pockets which formed around his right eye, the UFC arranged for Koscheck and his trainer to drive to [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), where Koscheck received corrective surgery.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

#### Post-title shot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Josh_Koscheck&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Post-title shot")]

Koscheck defeated former UFC welterweight champion [Matt Hughes](/wiki/Matt_Hughes_(fighter) "Matt Hughes (fighter)") on September 24, 2011 at [UFC 135](/wiki/UFC_135 "UFC 135") via KO in the first round, replacing an injured [Diego Sanchez](/wiki/Diego_Sanchez "Diego Sanchez").[[18]](#cite_note-18) This fight earned him a $75,000 *[Knockout of the Night](/wiki/List_of_UFC_bonus_award_recipients "List of UFC bonus award recipients")* award.[[19]](#cite_note-UFC135-19)

Koscheck was expected to face [Carlos Condit](/wiki/Carlos_Condit "Carlos Condit") on February 4, 2012 at [UFC 143](/wiki/UFC_143 "UFC 143").[[20]](#cite_note-20) However, on December 7, 2011, it was revealed [Georges St-Pierre](/wiki/Georges_St-Pierre "Georges St-Pierre") had sustained ACL injury to his left knee and the injury would sideline him for around ten months, forcing him out of his scheduled bout with [Nick Diaz](/wiki/Nick_Diaz "Nick Diaz").[[21]](#cite_note-21) Condit and Diaz faced each other at the event with the winner crowned interim [UFC Welterweight Champion](/wiki/UFC_Welterweight_Championship "UFC Welterweight Championship"), while Koscheck faced [Mike Pierce](/wiki/Mike_Pierce "Mike Pierce") at the event.[[22]](#cite_note-22) He won the fight via split decision.

Koscheck then faced [Johny Hendricks](/wiki/Johny_Hendricks "Johny Hendricks") on May 5, 2012 at [UFC on Fox 3](/wiki/UFC_on_Fox_3 "UFC on Fox 3").[[23]](#cite_note-23) He lost the fight via split decision.

Koscheck was expected to face [Jake Ellenberger](/wiki/Jake_Ellenberger "Jake Ellenberger") on September 1, 2012 at [UFC 151](/wiki/UFC_151 "UFC 151").[[24]](#cite_note-24) However, Koscheck pulled out of the bout citing a back injury.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Koscheck faced returning veteran [Robbie Lawler](/wiki/Robbie_Lawler "Robbie Lawler") on February 23, 2013 at [UFC 157](/wiki/UFC_157 "UFC 157").[[26]](#cite_note-26) Despite being able to take down Lawler several times during the fight, Koscheck lost by TKO after being rocked by a knee to the head while shooting for a takedown, then finished with punches on the ground by Lawler at 3:57 on the 1st round.

Koscheck was expected to face [Demian Maia](/wiki/Demian_Maia "Demian Maia") on August 3, 2013 at [UFC 163](/wiki/UFC_163 "UFC 163").[[27]](#cite_note-27) However, Koscheck was forced out of the bout with an injury and as a result, Maia was pulled from the card as well.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Koscheck next faced [Tyron Woodley](/wiki/Tyron_Woodley "Tyron Woodley") at [UFC 167](/wiki/UFC_167 "UFC 167") on November 16, 2013.[[29]](#cite_note-29) He lost the fight via knockout in the first round.

Koscheck was briefly linked to a bout with [Neil Magny](/wiki/Neil_Magny "Neil Magny") at [UFC 184](/wiki/UFC_184 "UFC 184") on February 28, 2015.[[30]](#cite_note-30) However, the pairing was cancelled and Koscheck instead faced [Jake Ellenberger](/wiki/Jake_Ellenberger "Jake Ellenberger") at the event.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Koscheck lost the fight via submission in the second round.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Just 21 days after his loss to Ellenberger, Koscheck replaced an injured [Ben Saunders](/wiki/Ben_Saunders_(fighter) "Ben Saunders (fighter)") to face [Erick Silva](/wiki/Erick_Silva "Erick Silva") on March 21, 2015 at [UFC Fight Night 62](/wiki/UFC_Fight_Night_62 "UFC Fight Night 62").[[33]](#cite_note-33) Koscheck lost the fight by submission in the first round.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Bellator MMA

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Josh_Koscheck&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Bellator MMA")]

On June 26, 2015, it was announced that Koscheck signed a multi-year deal with [Bellator MMA](/wiki/Bellator_MMA "Bellator MMA").[[35]](#cite_note-35) Koscheck was set to make his debut against fellow welterweight Matthew Secor on January 29, 2016 in his home town of Fresno, California and was set to also be the main event.

It was announced on January 31, 2017, that Koscheck's previously delayed Bellator debut would take place at Bellator 172 on February 18, 2017 against Mauricio Alonso.[[36]](#cite_note-36) He lost the fight via TKO in the first round.

Koscheck officially announced his retirement from MMA on June 28, 2018.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Josh_Koscheck&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Koscheck attended and graduated from [Edinboro University](/wiki/Edinboro_University "Edinboro University") with a [bachelor's degree](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree") in [criminal justice](/wiki/Criminal_justice "Criminal justice"), becoming the first member of his family to obtain a college degree.

Koscheck has been married to Tiaira Nowlin, a realty agent, since 2017.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)
